content.text: Cambridge International Examinations are the cornerstone of academic credibility for Eduversal Partner Schools. Maintaining examination integrity, security, and fairness is not only a contractual requirement of our Cambridge Centre status but also a demonstration of our commitment to ethical assessment and international standards. This section provides a comprehensive operational framework for managing all Cambridge assessments—from Checkpoint through IGCSE to AS and A Level—ensuring compliance with the Cambridge Handbook for Centres, protecting candidate welfare, and upholding the validity of qualifications issued. Key Objectives:

items: Ensure secure, fair, and compliant administration of all Cambridge examinations
Maintain Cambridge Centre status through adherence to regulations and annual declarations
Protect candidate welfare and rights throughout the examination process
Provide accurate, timely data to inform teaching, intervention, and university counseling
Demonstrate assessment integrity to meet CIS/WASC accreditation standards
CIS Domain: Teaching & Assessing for Learning (Assessment Integrity)
WASC Category: B. Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment; C. Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth
Cambridge: Centre Handbook compliance; Quality Management Standards
Indonesian Context: Integration with rapor (report cards); data reporting to Dapodik, where applicable

content.text: (See Section 3: Cambridge Centre Compliance for Centre registration and Quality Nominee responsibilities; Section 6: Assessment, Moderation, and Reporting for internal exam design; Section 8: Inclusion, EAL, and Learning Support for access arrangements.)
Clear role delineation ensures accountability and prevents gaps in examination management.
content.title: 10.2.1 Roles and Responsibilities

content.text: The Principal holds ultimate accountability for Cambridge examinations and serves as the named Head of Centre on Cambridge records. Key Responsibilities:

items: Sign and submit the Cambridge Centre Annual Declaration by the deadline (typically March)
Approve examination entries, timetable clashes, and special consideration requests
Ensure adequate secure storage facilities and monitor key control logs
Authorize appointment of the Exams Officer and Quality Nominee
Oversee investigations of malpractice or maladministration and communicate with Cambridge
Review post-results analysis and integrate findings into the School Improvement Plan (SIP)
Allocate budget for examination fees, invigilation costs, and contingency arrangements
Report examination outcomes to the Board and integrate data into EduNav dashboards
content.title: Principal

items: Signed Centre Annual Declaration (stored in secure digital repository)
Board reports on examination outcomes and trends
Exam budget allocations and expenditure records

content.text: The Exams Officer is the operational lead for all examination logistics, working closely with Cambridge and internal teams. Key Responsibilities: Before Examinations:

items: Manage the examination entry process: verify student eligibility, submit entries via Cambridge International Direct (CID), and confirm fee payments
Create and publish the internal examination timetable, resolving timetable clashes using Cambridge-approved methods (rest breaks, separate sittings)
Coordinate access arrangements with the Learning Support Team (See Section 8.4)
Order and receive question papers and materials per the Cambridge Key Times schedule
Conduct secure storage audits and maintain control logs for all materials
Train and brief invigilators using standardized checklists and scenario-based training

Prepare examination rooms: seating plans (minimum 1.25m spacing in all directions, measured from the center of one candidate's desk to the next), display materials (clocks, notices, candidate information), check environmental conditions (lighting, ventilation)
Prepare the Cambridge Centre Annual Declaration, ensuring all compliance checks are complete, for the Principal's review and signature
content.title: Exams Officer

items: Supervise the opening of question paper packets per Cambridge instructions (verify seals, count papers, record serial numbers)
Monitor invigilation quality through unannounced room visits
Manage examination incidents (fire alarms, medical emergencies, suspected malpractice) using the Incident Response Protocol (Template 10A)
Coordinate special consideration requests for illness, bereavement, or other adverse circumstances
Ensure secure dispatch of scripts to Cambridge within 48 hours (or per specification)
Submit special consideration and access arrangements reports via CID
Collate and analyze the results data upon release
Manage post-results services (enquiries about results, access to scripts, remarks)
Maintain examination records for the required retention period (minimum 2 years)

content.text: Line of Accountability: Reports to the Principal; collaborates with Heads of Department, IT Coordinator, and Facilities Manager. Evidence/Artifacts:

items: CID entry records and confirmation receipts
Secure storage audit logs (monthly checks)
Invigilator training attendance sheets and signed declarations
Incident reports and Cambridge correspondence
Post-results service requests and outcomes

content.text: Invigilators are the frontline guardians of examination integrity. They must be appropriately trained, vigilant, and fair. Selection Criteria:

items: Not related to candidates in the examination
Not teaching the subject being examined or year groups (where possible)
Passed safeguarding checks (See Section 9: Safeguarding)
Completed annual invigilation training and signed the Code of Conduct
content.title: Invigilators

items: Before the exam: Arrive 30 minutes early; collect materials, seating plans, and attendance registers; verify room setup
During the exam:
Read the Examination Instructions to Candidates script verbatim
Monitor candidate behavior continuously; document any irregularities on the Invigilator Report Form (Template 10B)
Assist candidates with permitted queries (timing, materials) without providing subject-specific help
Enforce prohibited items policy (phones, smartwatches, unauthorized notes)
Ensure silence and fairness throughout the session
After the exam: Complete attendance registers and incident reports; return all materials to the Exams Officer; maintain confidentiality.
Annual refresher training covering:
Cambridge examination regulations
Identification of malpractice (plagiarism, collusion, unauthorized materials)
Special consideration and access arrangements awareness
Emergency procedures (fire, medical incident, bomb threat)
Resources: Invigilation Handbook (Template 10C), scenario-based e-learning module via EduNav
Signed Invigilator Declarations
Training logs and certificates
Completed Invigilator Report Forms

content.text: The Quality Nominee is Cambridge's designated contact for quality assurance and compliance matters. Typically, this is a senior academic leader (Vice Principal, Director of Studies, or experienced HoD). Key Responsibilities:

items: Liaise with Cambridge on Centre inspections or audits
Lead internal quality assurance reviews (moderation, marking accuracy, procedures)
Ensure syllabus changes and assessment updates are communicated to teaching staff
Support the Principal and Exams Officer during malpractice investigations
Participate in Cambridge training webinars and share updates with the team
content.title: Quality Nominee

content.text: (See Section 3.3 for Quality Nominee appointment and annual review requirements.)
The Board of Governors receives an Annual Examinations Report each November, covering:

items: Examination participation rates and outcomes (pass rates, grade distributions, trends)
Cambridge Centre compliance status and any notices received
Incident summary (malpractice cases, maladministration, appeals)
Budget vs. actual expenditure for examination fees and logistics
Recommendations for continuous improvement
content.title: 10.2.2 Governance Oversight

content.text: Evidence/Artifact: Annual Examinations Report to Board (Template 10D)
Cambridge examination materials must be stored in conditions that prevent unauthorized access, tampering, or environmental damage. Physical Security:

items: Dedicated secure room or cabinet with:
Steel construction or equivalent
Dual locks (e.g., key \+ combination or two separate keys held by different individuals)
Sealed access log mounted on the exterior
Environmental controls: Temperature 15-25°C, humidity \<70%, fire suppression system or proximity to fire extinguishers
Access restrictions: Only the Principal and Exams Officer hold keys; any access requires dual authorization and log entry
content.title: 10.3.1 Secure Storage Requirements

items: Devices storing Cambridge assessment materials (e.g., on-screen exams) must have:
Encrypted storage and password protection
Network isolation during examinations (no internet access except where specified by Cambridge)
Antivirus and anti-tampering software updated before exam sessions
Conduct a visual inspection of the storage area
Verify lock functionality and seal integrity
Review access logs for anomalies
Report findings to Principal via Secure Storage Audit Form (Template 10E)
Secure storage access logs (digital or physical ledger)
Monthly audit reports
Photos of secure storage setup (for Cambridge Centre inspections)

content.text: Cambridge publishes a Key Times schedule for each examination series. Adherence to these deadlines is mandatory. Critical Milestones: ![][image2] Materials Receipt Process:

steps: Verify the courier's identity and sign the delivery receipt
Inspect packages for tampering (check seals, count parcels against packing list)
Log receipt in the Secure Storage Register with date, time, and condition notes
Store immediately in the secure room; do NOT open question paper packets until exam day
content.title: 10.3.2 Key Times and Materials Handling

steps: Pack scripts securely in Cambridge-provided envelopes or approved containers
Complete dispatch documentation (manifest, special consideration forms if applicable)
Photograph sealed packages before handover (quality assurance)
Use a tracked courier service with signature confirmation
Retain proof of dispatch for audit trail

items: Courier receipts and tracking numbers
Secure Storage Register entries
Dispatch photos and manifests
[ ] Confirm room bookings and display "EXAM IN PROGRESS" signage
[ ] Prepare seating plans with candidate names/numbers (alphabetical or by class)
[ ] Set up examination desks (minimum 1.25m spacing in all directions, measured from the center of one candidate's desk to the next; check against Cambridge spacing diagrams)
[ ] Verify clock synchronization (all clocks display the same time; visible to all candidates)
[ ] Test environmental conditions (temperature, lighting, noise levels)
[ ] Place candidate instructions posters, including the 'Warning to Candidates,' 'Notice to Candidates,' and the centre's 'Emergency Procedures' notice
[ ] Prepare invigilator packs (attendance registers, report forms, spare stationery, stopwatch)
content.title: 10.4.1 Pre-Examination Setup (Day Before)

items: [ ] Test all devices (laptops, tablets) and ensure battery charge \>80%
[ ] Load assessment software and verify network isolation
[ ] Prepare backup devices (10% of cohort size as spares)
[ ] Test printer functionality for on-demand printing of scripts

content.text: Opening the Exam (15 minutes before start time):

steps: Invigilators arrive and collect materials from the Exams Officer
Candidates admitted to the exam room; seating verified against plan
Candidate identity checks (school ID, photo verification if unfamiliar)
Confiscate prohibited items (phones, notes, smartwatches); store securely or ask candidates to leave outside the room
Announce exam details using the Examination Instructions to Candidates script (duration, permitted materials, bathroom procedures)
Open question paper packets in front of candidates (verify seals, count papers)
Distribute question papers and answer booklets face down
"You may begin" announcement at the official start time; start the clock
content.title: 10.4.2 During the Examination

items: Continuous vigilance: Invigilators patrol the room quietly, observing candidate behavior

Permitted assistance: Clarify timing, allow bathroom visits (one candidate at a time, who must be accompanied by an invigilator to the door of the facility), provide replacement materials (pens, answer booklets)
Document irregularities: Any suspected malpractice (whispering, unauthorized materials, copying) must be noted on the Invigilator Report Form with timestamps and seating position
Do NOT accuse candidates during the exam; remain calm and neutral; investigate afterward
15 minutes remaining: "You have 15 minutes left."
5 minutes remaining: "You have 5 minutes left; check that you have written your name and candidate number on all answer sheets."
"Stop writing" command: At the end of the time, instruct candidates to put down their pens immediately

steps: Collect all materials (question papers, answer booklets, rough work) systematically (row by row or desk by desk)
Count scripts against the attendance register; investigate discrepancies immediately
Dismiss candidates row by row to avoid congestion
Invigilators complete the Attendance Register and the Invigilator Report Form
Return all materials to the Exams Officer within 30 minutes

items: Completed Attendance Registers
Invigilator Report Forms (even if "no irregularities")
Photos of room setup (for quality assurance)

content.text: When a candidate is entered for two examinations scheduled at the same time, Cambridge permits the following clash resolution methods: Option 1: Different Start Times (Supervised Rest Break)

items: The candidate sits Exam A at the normal time
Supervised rest break (maximum 20 minutes) in a secure room (no contact with other candidates, no access to phones/notes)
The candidate sits Exam B immediately after the rest break
The Exams Officer must approve and document the arrangement
content.title: 10.4.3 Managing Timetable Clashes

items: The candidate takes both exams simultaneously in a separate room
Extended time may be required to complete both papers
An additional invigilator is required for the separate room
Complete the Timetable Clash Report (Template 10F) and retain for audit
No advance permission from Cambridge is required for clashes resolved using approved methods

content.text: Despite the best planning, incidents may occur. The Incident Response Protocol ensures swift, appropriate action. Common Incidents: ![][image3] Evidence/Artifacts:

items: Incident Report Form (Template 10G) completed within 24 hours
Photos or confiscated materials (for malpractice cases)
Correspondence with Cambridge (for guidance or special consideration)
content.title: 10.4.4 Examination Incidents and Emergencies

content.text: Malpractice: Any act by a candidate that undermines the integrity of the examination or gives an unfair advantage. Examples include:

items: Plagiarism: Submitting work copied from another source without attribution
Collusion: Unauthorized collaboration on coursework or controlled assessments
Prohibited materials: Possession of phones, notes, or other unauthorized items during an exam
Disruptive behavior: Deliberate disturbance or refusal to follow instructions
Impersonation: Sitting an exam on behalf of another candidate
content.title: 10.5.1 Definitions

content.text: Maladministration: Any act by Centre staff (teachers, invigilators, administrators) that compromises examination security or fairness. Examples include:

items: Opening question papers early or allowing unauthorized access to secure materials
Providing unauthorized assistance to candidates during exams
Failing to report suspected malpractice
Inadequate supervision or missing invigilation procedures
Falsifying attendance or special consideration claims
Failing to provide approved access arrangements, or conversely, providing access arrangements to a candidate without formal Cambridge approval
The invigilator or staff member completes the Incident Report Form
Evidence collected (confiscated materials, witness statements, CCTV footage if available)
Report submitted to Exams Officer and Principal
content.title: 10.5.2 Investigation Process

items: The Principal and Exams Officer review the evidence
If minor (e.g., accidental phone in bag, not switched on): Recorded as a warning; no further action
If serious (e.g., active use of phone, notes, copying): Proceed to formal investigation
Candidate and parent/guardian invited to a meeting (with 48 hours' notice)
Evidence presented; candidate given opportunity to respond
Meeting documented on Malpractice Investigation Notes (Template 10H)

content.text: Step 4: Decision and Report to Cambridge (5-7 days)

items: The principal determines if malpractice occurred
If malpractice confirmed: Complete the Cambridge Report of Suspected Malpractice form (JF code) via CID
Cambridge reviews the case and determines sanctions (warning, grade penalty, disqualification, ban from future exams)
School sanctions (if applicable): Aligned with Student Conduct Policy (See Section 22)

content.text: Step 5: Communication and Record Keeping (7-10 days)

items: Inform the candidate and parent of outcome in writing
File all documentation in secure student record
Update Malpractice Log (Template 10I) for annual reporting to Board
Incident Reports and Investigation Notes
Candidate statements and supporting documents
Cambridge correspondence and sanction outcomes
Malpractice Log (confidential; stored securely for 6 years)

content.text: Access arrangements provide pre-exam support to candidates with documented learning needs, ensuring they can demonstrate their knowledge without unfair advantage or disadvantage. (Detailed procedures covered in Section 8: Inclusion, EAL, and Learning Support. Key points summarized here for examination context.) Common Access Arrangements:

items: Extra time (typically 25% additional)
Separate invigilation in a smaller room
Reader or scribe support (for candidates with specific learning difficulties)
Modified question papers (enlarged text, Braille, colored overlays)
Rest breaks (for medical conditions requiring breaks)
Word processor use (for candidates unable to handwrite)
content.title: 10.6.1 Access Arrangements

steps: The Learning Support Team identifies eligible candidates based on diagnostic assessments or medical documentation
Submit application to Cambridge via CID by the March deadline (May/June series)
Receive approval from Cambridge (or proceed with Centre-approved arrangements per Cambridge guidance)
Inform Exams Officer to arrange room setup, invigilators, and materials

items: Diagnostic reports (educational psychologist, medical professional)
Cambridge access arrangements approval confirmations
Record of access arrangements used (stored in candidate file)

content.text: (See Section 8.4 for full Access Arrangements Policy and Template 8C: Access Arrangements Request Form.)

Special consideration provides post-exam grade adjustments for candidates whose performance was affected by temporary illness, injury, bereavement, or other adverse circumstances on the day of the exam. Eligibility Criteria:

items: Candidate was present and completed the exam (or made a genuine attempt)
Adverse circumstances were beyond the candidate's control
Evidence (medical certificate, death certificate) is available and submitted within 7 days of the exam
content.title: 10.6.2 Special Consideration

items: Illness on exam day (fever, injury, severe headache) with medical certificate
Bereavement (death of immediate family member within 1 month of exam)
Accident or trauma (car accident en route to exam, witnessed violence)
Centre-related issues (fire alarm interruption \>15 minutes, heating failure)
Long-term conditions already supported via access arrangements
Minor ailments (mild cold, exam stress)
Circumstances known in advance (family events, voluntary activities)

steps: Candidate or parent reports adverse circumstances to Exams Officer within 24 hours
Collect evidence (medical certificate, incident report)
Exams Officer submits special consideration request via CID within 7 days of the exam
Cambridge reviews the evidence and, if approved, may apply a small percentage adjustment to the candidate's mark. The exact adjustment is at Cambridge's discretion and is not disclosed.
Inform candidate of outcome when results are released

items: Medical certificates or supporting documents
CID special consideration submission receipts
Special Consideration Log (Template 10J)

content.text: When Cambridge releases results (mid-August for IGCSE/AS/A Level; early November for Checkpoint), the Exams Officer leads a comprehensive data analysis to inform teaching, intervention, and strategic planning. Results Analysis Protocol: Step 1: Data Collection (Results Day)

items: Download results from Cambridge International Direct (CID)
Upload to EduNav for automated dashboard generation
Distribute individual results to candidates (via secure portal or hard copy)
content.title: 10.7.1 Results Analysis and Reporting

items: Heads of Department receive Subject Performance Reports (Template 10K) showing:
Grade distribution (A-E) vs. Cambridge global benchmarks
Component analysis (Paper 1 vs. Paper 2 vs. Coursework)
Comparison with predicted grades (from Mock exams)
Identification of topics/skills with low performance
Subject review meetings to discuss:
Strengths to celebrate
Gaps requiring curriculum or teaching adjustments
Student intervention needs for resits or next level
Senior Leadership Team reviews Whole-School Performance Dashboard (EduNav) covering:
Overall pass rates and average grades
Progress of subgroups (EAL, SEN, gender, socioeconomic status)
Year-on-year trends (3-year rolling data)
University entrance outcomes (% meeting offers)
Comparative analysis against:
Eduversal network average (benchmark across 15+ schools)
Cambridge global averages (published by Cambridge for each subject)
School targets (set in the School Improvement Plan)

content.text: Step 4: Integration into School Improvement Plan (Week 3)

items: Action planning meetings with Heads of Department:
Identify professional development needs (e.g., training on Paper 2 extended writing)
Plan curriculum adjustments (e.g., add more practice on specific topics)
Set targets for next cohort (e.g., increase A/A grades by 5%)
Update SIP with examination-driven priorities (See Section 4: Strategic Planning)
Subject Performance Reports (Template 10K)
EduNav dashboards (exported as PDFs for records)
SIP amendments and action plans
Professional development requests linked to results analysis

content.text: Cambridge offers several post-results services to address queries or concerns about grades. The Exams Officer manages these requests in consultation with candidates, parents, and subject teachers. Service 1: Clerical Re-Check (Priority Service)

items: Purpose: Verify that all parts of the script were marked and totaled correctly
Outcome: No change to marks (unless addition error found); very rare
Cost: Approx. $30 per component
Deadline: Typically 3 weeks after results release
Recommendation: Use only if candidate believes sections were not marked
content.title: 10.7.2 Post-Results Services

items: Purpose: Re-mark by a senior examiner to check for marking errors
Outcome: Marks may go up, down, or stay the same (candidates must accept potential downgrade)
Cost: Approx. $100-$150 per component (refunded if grade changes)
Deadline: Typically 3 weeks after results release
Recommendation: Use when performance is significantly lower than expected (e.g., predicted A, achieved D) and internal moderation suggests marking error
Purpose: Obtain a copy of the candidate's marked script for review
Outcome: Useful for understanding examiner feedback; does NOT change the grade
Cost: Approx. $20 per component
Recommendation: Use for learning purposes (improve future performance) rather than grade disputes

steps: Candidate or parent requests post-results service
Subject teacher reviews candidate's performance data (internal exams, predicted grades, coursework)
HoD and Exams Officer discuss likelihood of grade change
School policy: School covers costs for clerical re-checks; candidates/parents pay for reviews of marking (refunded if grade improves)
Submit request via CID by the deadline
Communicate outcome to candidate and update records

items: Post-Results Service Request Forms (Template 10L)
Payment receipts and refund records
Outcome notifications from Cambridge
Cambridge dispatches examination certificates to the Centre approximately 3 months after results release (November for Aug results; February for Nov results)
Exams Officer verifies certificate accuracy against results data
Distribution methods:
Continuing students: Distributed during assemblies or form time
Alumni: Collected in person (with ID verification) or mailed via registered post
content.title: 10.7.3 Certificate Distribution and Record Keeping

items: Cambridge charges a fee (approx. $50) for duplicate certificates
Candidates complete Lost Certificate Request Form (Template 10M)
Processing time: 6-8 weeks

content.text: Record Retention: Per Cambridge and accreditation requirements:

items: Results data and certificates: Permanent digital archive (EduNav \+ secure cloud storage)

Unused question papers and unused answer booklets/scripts: Securely destroyed (e.g., cross-shredded) after the post-results service window has closed. A destruction log must be maintained. (typically 6 months post-results)
Access arrangements and special consideration documentation: 6 years
Malpractice records: 6 years (secure, confidential storage)
Certificate distribution logs (signature required)
Digital archive of results (backed up quarterly)
Destruction logs for question papers (with Principal authorization)

content.text: In the event of a significant disruption (e.g., pandemic, political unrest, natural disaster), the Examinations Business Continuity Plan (Template 10N) ensures minimal disruption to candidate welfare and qualification outcomes. Key Elements:

steps: Communication Tree: Rapid notification to candidates, parents, and Cambridge (within 2 hours)
Alternative Venues: Pre-arranged agreements with nearby Eduversal schools or public facilities
Remote Invigilation: Procedures for at-home examinations (if authorized by Cambridge)
Predicted Grades Submission: Robust internal assessment data to support Cambridge centre-assessed grades if exams are canceled
content.title: 10.8.2 Business Continuity for Examinations

content.text: (See Section 15: Risk Management, Emergency Preparedness, and Business Continuity for full institutional resilience framework.)
Each year, the Exams Officer and Quality Nominee lead a Post-Series Review (held in September for May/June series; March for Oct/Nov series). Review Components:

steps: Data Review: Analysis of results trends, post-results service outcomes, special consideration approvals
Stakeholder Feedback: Surveys of candidates, invigilators, and subject teachers (Template 10O)
Incident Analysis: Review of all incident reports and malpractice cases (anonymized for learning)
Compliance Audit: Self-assessment against Cambridge Centre Handbook requirements
Action Planning: Identify improvements for next series (training needs, procedure updates, resource allocation)
content.title: 10.9.1 Annual Review of Examination Procedures

items: Post-Series Review Report (Template 10P) submitted to Principal and Board
Updated SOPs for identified gaps or new Cambridge requirements
Professional Development Plan for Exams Officer and Invigilators
Budget recommendations for the next academic year
Post-Series Review Report
Stakeholder survey results and analysis
Updated SOPs with version control and change log
Training calendar for next academic year

content.text: Cambridge may conduct announced or unannounced inspections to verify Centre compliance. The school maintains inspection readiness year-round. Inspection Readiness Checklist:

items: [ ] All secure storage logs up to date (no gaps or unsigned entries)
[ ] Invigilator training records, complete with signatures
[ ] Access arrangements documentation filed and cross-referenced to candidates
[ ] Examination room photos and seating plans archived
[ ] Malpractice investigations documented with Cambridge correspondence
[ ] Annual Declaration signed and submitted on time
[ ] Quality Nominee active participation in Cambridge webinars (certificates on file)
content.title: 10.9.2 Cambridge Centre Inspections

steps: Welcome inspector and provide workspace (private office with access to files)
Quality Nominee leads response to inspector requests
Provide evidence promptly and transparently (digital files via secure share; physical documents with sign-out log)
Conduct an exit interview to understand preliminary findings
Action plan response to any recommendations within the required timeframe (typically 28 days)

items: Share inspection report with Board and Senior Leadership Team
Integrate recommendations into School Improvement Plan
Monitor implementation of corrective actions via EduNav task tracker
Inspection reports and correspondence
Action plan responses and completion evidence
Board minutes discussing inspection outcomes

content.text: Clear and timely communication reduces anxiety and ensures candidates are well-prepared for their examinations. Communication Timeline: ![][image5] Communication Principles:

items: Bilingual: All critical communications in English and Bahasa Indonesia (See Section 16: Parent, Student, and Community Engagement)
Accessible: Plain language, visual aids (infographics, timelines), and EAL-friendly formatting
Multi-channel: Email, SMS, website, student portal, and print for maximum reach
Transparent: Honest about timelines, costs, and limitations (e.g., "Cambridge results cannot be appealed directly by parents")
content.title: 10.10.1 Communication to Candidates and Parents

items: Communication log (dates, recipients, content)
Parent information session attendance records and presentation slides
Sample communications in both languages
Candidate feedback surveys on the clarity of information
Receive examination entry lists by December to verify candidate eligibility
Attend examiner training (Cambridge-led webinars or recorded sessions) to understand mark schemes
Provide predicted grades for university applications (based on internal assessments and Mock results)
Support post-results analysis by reviewing component performance data
content.title: 10.10.2 Internal Communication (Staff)

items: Monthly updates from Exams Officer on entry progress, timetable development, and risk issues
Approval required for significant decisions (late entries, withdrawal of candidates, malpractice sanctions)
Budget oversight for examination fees and contingency expenses
Termly updates on examination preparation (via Principal's report)
Annual Examinations Report (post-results) covering outcomes, compliance, and recommendations
SLT meeting minutes with examination agenda items
Board reports and approval records
Teacher training attendance logs

content.text: Cambridge charges fees per subject per candidate, with costs varying by qualification level and series. Typical Fee Ranges (2025-26 Academic Year):

items: Cambridge Checkpoint (Year 6/8): $30-50 per subject
IGCSE: $100-150 per subject
AS Level: $120-180 per subject
A Level: $150-220 per subject
Late entry surcharge: \+30% of the subject fee
Post-results services: $30-150 per component
content.title: 10.11.1 Examination Fee Structure

items: Standard entries: Included in school tuition fees (parents do not pay separately)
Late entries: Parent responsibility (if due to student/parent delay)
Resits or additional subjects: Parent responsibility
Post-results services: Parent responsibility (refunded if grade changes)

content.text: The Exams Officer prepares an Annual Examination Budget (Template 10Q) each March for the following academic year, based on:

items: Projected enrollment by qualification level
Subject choices from option blocks
Contingency for late entries (5% of total)
Invigilation costs (substitution coverage for exam-day staff)
Secure storage maintenance and upgrades
Training and professional development
content.title: 10.11.2 Annual Examination Budget

steps: Exams Officer submits draft budget to Principal (March)
Principal reviews and integrates into whole-school budget (See Section 13: Operations, Finance, and Resources)
Board approves final budget (April Board meeting)
Quarterly expenditure reviews to monitor actuals vs. projections

items: Annual Examination Budget with line-item breakdown
Quarterly budget vs. actual reports
Board approval minutes

content.text: The following templates support standardized, efficient examination management. All templates are version-controlled and available via EduNav \> Resources \> Examinations Template ![][image6] ![][image7] Management:

items: Version control: Each template includes a version number, an effective date, and a change log
Annual review: Templates reviewed during Post-Series Review and updated as needed
Accessibility: Templates available in Microsoft Word and Google Docs formats; bilingual where appropriate

content.text: The Exams Officer is required to maintain current knowledge of Cambridge regulations and best practices. Mandatory Training:

items: Cambridge Exams Officer Course (online or in-person) within 6 months of appointment
Annual refresher webinars offered by Cambridge (minimum 2 per year)
Eduversal network training (cross-school sharing of best practices, held twice annually)
content.title: 10.13.1 Exams Officer Training

items: Data analysis training for results interpretation and dashboard use
Project management for managing multiple examination series concurrently
Stakeholder communication for parent/candidate engagement
Training certificates and attendance records
Professional development log in EduNav
Application of learning documented in Post-Series Review

content.text: All invigilators complete annual training before the examination series begins. Training Content (3-hour session):

steps: Welcome and overview (15 min): Role importance, legal responsibilities
Cambridge regulations (45 min): Examination instructions, permitted materials, prohibited items, special arrangements
Scenario-based practice (60 min): Handling late arrivals, suspected malpractice, medical emergencies, and technology failures
Documentation requirements (30 min): Completing attendance registers, incident reports, and confidentiality
Q\&A and assessment (30 min): Knowledge check quiz (80% pass required)
content.title: 10.13.2 Invigilator Training Program

items: 2-hour update session covering regulation changes and lessons learned from previous series
Review of incident reports from the last series (anonymized case studies)
Training attendance sheets with signatures
Quiz results (filed in HR records)
Signed the Invigilator Code of Conduct declarations

content.text: Examination data is integrated into EduNav to provide real-time visibility and support data-driven decision-making. Key Dashboards:

steps: Exams Officer Dashboard:
content.title: 10.14.1 EduNav Dashboard Integration

items: Entry progress tracker (entries submitted vs. pending)
Secure storage audit status (green/amber/red indicators)
Upcoming deadlines (auto-alerts 7 days before)
Budget vs. actual expenditure

steps: Principal Dashboard:

items: Results overview (pass rates, average grades, trends)
Compliance status (Centre Annual Declaration, training completion rates)
Risk indicators (incident frequency, malpractice cases)

steps: HoD Dashboard:

items: Subject performance data (component analysis, comparison with predictions)
Student intervention tracker (candidates requiring support for resits)
Access arrangements status (applications pending, approved)

steps: Parent/Student Portal:

items: Individual examination timetable
Results notification (when released by Cambridge)
Post-results services status tracker
Entries: Uploaded to EduNav from Cambridge International Direct (CID) API integration
Results: Imported from CID and distributed via secure portal
Performance analytics: Auto-generated upon results import; updated with Mock exam data for predictive insights

content.text: Examination management practices align closely with the whole-school Assessment Policy (See Section 6: Assessment, Moderation, and Reporting). Key Alignments: ![][image8]
content.title: 10.14.2 Assessment Policy Alignment

content.text: The school monitors examination management effectiveness using the following KPIs, reported quarterly to the Board and updated in EduNav. Reporting:

items: Quarterly KPI Dashboard (EduNav) reviewed by SLT
Annual KPI Summary included in the Annual Examinations Report to the Board
Action plans are developed for any KPI falling below the target

content.text: This section directly supports the following accreditation standards and regulatory requirements:

items: Teaching & Assessing for Learning: Assessment integrity, fairness, validity, and reliability
Operational Systems: Secure materials management, record keeping, risk mitigation
Student Wellbeing: Candidate support, stress management, access to special arrangements
content.title: CIS Standards:

items: B. Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment: Valid and reliable assessment of student learning; alignment with curriculum
C. Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth: Access arrangements for diverse learners; examination stress support
D. Resource Management and Development: Adequate facilities, budget allocation, and staffing for examination administration
E. School Culture and Environment for Learning: Ethical culture, academic integrity, fairness
content.title: WASC Categories:

items: Full compliance with the Cambridge Handbook for Centres (latest edition)
Timely submission of the Centre Annual Declaration
Quality Nominee active engagement with Cambridge support
Secure storage meeting Cambridge specifications
Malpractice prevention and reporting protocols
content.title: Cambridge Centre Requirements:

items: Integration of Cambridge results into rapor (report cards) per Permendikbud requirements
Data reporting to Dapodik (where applicable for nationally-recognized curricula)
Compliance with student data protection norms
content.title: Indonesian Regulations:

content.text: Evidence for Accreditation Visits: All templates, dashboards, logs, and reports listed in this section are maintained as accreditation evidence and organized in the Accreditation Evidence Repository (EduNav \> Accreditation \> Cambridge Examinations).
This policy is reviewed annually by the Exams Officer, Quality Nominee, and Principal following the completion of the October/November examination series. Review Process:

steps: Post-Series Review Report (Section 10.9.1) provides the data foundation
Stakeholder feedback incorporated from surveys and focus groups
Cambridge updates (regulation changes, new guidance) integrated
Lessons learned from incident reports and malpractice cases analyzed
Draft revisions circulated to Senior Leadership Team for input
Final approval by Principal and Board (January Board meeting)
Updated policy published on EduNav and communicated to all staff

items: Current version: 1.0 (Effective January 2026)
Next review date: January 2027
Change log: Maintained at end of document with date, section changed, rationale, and approver

content.text: Effective Cambridge examinations management is a hallmark of a high-performing international school. By adhering to the procedures, standards, and safeguards outlined in this section, Eduversal Partner Schools demonstrate:

items: Unwavering commitment to examination integrity and fairness
Respect for candidates' rights to accurate, valid assessment of their learning
Accountability to stakeholders (students, parents, universities, Cambridge, accreditors)
Continuous improvement driven by data, feedback, and reflection

content.text: The Principal, Exams Officer, Quality Nominee, and all examination personnel are entrusted with upholding these standards. Their professionalism, vigilance, and dedication ensure that every Cambridge certificate issued reflects genuine achievement and opens doors to future success. ## Appendices

items: Section 3: Cambridge Centre Compliance (Centre registration, Quality Nominee role, annual declarations)
Section 6: Assessment, Moderation, and Reporting (internal assessment alignment, predicted grades, academic integrity)
Section 8: Inclusion, EAL, and Learning Support (access arrangements procedures, SEN documentation, MTSS framework)
Section 9: Safeguarding, Child Protection, and Wellbeing (candidate welfare during exams, stress support, safe spaces)
Section 13: Operations, Finance, and Resources (examination budget integration, facilities management)
Section 15: Risk Management and Emergency Preparedness (examination-specific risks, business continuity)
Section 17: Quality Assurance and Internal Review (examination results as QA data, benchmarking, action planning)
Section 18: Accreditation Toolkit (evidence mapping for CIS/WASC, self-study documentation)
content.title: Appendix 10D: Cross-References to Other Handbook Sections

content.text: This section is designed to be a living document, updated annually to reflect Cambridge regulation changes, school context evolution, and continuous improvement insights. All staff involved in examination management should familiarize themselves with this section and consult it regularly throughout the academic year.
